questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: Who created UNIX and at which laboratory?
questions.answer: Ken Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and others at Bell Labs (AT&T) in 1969.
questions.explanation: UNIX was developed at Bell Labs as a multi‑user, multi‑tasking operating system.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What was the main reason for rewriting UNIX in C?
questions.answer: To make the system portable across different hardware architectures.
questions.explanation: Originally written in assembly, rewriting in C allowed UNIX to be moved to new machines with minimal changes.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What is the significance of BSD (Berkeley Software Distribution)?
questions.answer: A major fork of UNIX developed at UC Berkeley that added TCP/IP, vi editor, and other tools.
questions.explanation: Many features of modern Unix systems (including sockets) came from BSD.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: Who created Linux and when?
questions.answer: Linus Torvalds in 1991.
questions.explanation: Linux started as a personal project to create a free, open‑source UNIX‑like kernel.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What is the GNU project and its goal?
questions.answer: Started by Richard Stallman in 1983 to create a complete free UNIX‑like operating system.
questions.explanation: GNU developed many essential tools (gcc, bash, coreutils) that are used with the Linux kernel.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What is the POSIX standard?
questions.answer: A family of standards for maintaining compatibility between operating systems.
questions.explanation: Most Unix‑like systems (Linux, macOS, BSD) comply with POSIX.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What was the first version of Linux kernel released?
questions.answer: 0.01, released in September 1991.
questions.explanation: It was not fully functional; version 1.0 came in 1994.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: Why is Linux often called a 'Unix‑like' system?
questions.answer: It follows Unix design principles, uses POSIX standards, and behaves similarly, but does not contain original Unix source code.
questions.explanation: Linux was written from scratch but intentionally mimics Unix semantics.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What was the role of AT&T in Unix commercialization?
questions.answer: AT&T licensed Unix to universities and commercial vendors, leading to many variants (AIX, HP-UX, Solaris).
questions.explanation: The licensing was later the subject of legal disputes between AT&T and BSD.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What is the difference between System V and BSD Unix?
questions.answer: System V (AT&T) and BSD (Berkeley) were two major Unix branches with different init systems, file system layouts, and command options.
questions.explanation: Many modern systems (Linux, macOS) have features from both.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: What is the core difference between Unix and Linux?
questions.answer: Unix is a family of proprietary operating systems; Linux is a free, open‑source kernel (often used with GNU tools).
questions.explanation: Linux is Unix‑like but not certified Unix (except some distributions that pay for certification).

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: Why do some people call the OS 'GNU/Linux' instead of just 'Linux'?
questions.answer: To credit the GNU project for supplying essential system tools (shell, compiler, libraries) without which the kernel alone is not a full OS.
questions.explanation: The term emphasises that the system is a combination of the Linux kernel and GNU utilities.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: Is macOS a Unix system?
questions.answer: Yes, macOS is a certified Unix (based on Darwin, which includes BSD components).
questions.explanation: Apple obtained official UNIX 03 certification for macOS.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: What is a 'Linux distribution' (distro)?
questions.answer: A complete operating system built around the Linux kernel, adding system libraries, package manager, desktop environment, and applications.
questions.explanation: Examples: Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, RHEL.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: Can Linux run Unix programs?
questions.answer: Yes, most Unix programs can be recompiled for Linux; Linux also has compatibility layers (e.g., `compat‑*` libraries).
questions.explanation: Linux aims to be highly POSIX‑compliant, making source‑level compatibility easy.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: What is the Single UNIX Specification (SUS)?
questions.answer: A standard that defines the interface and behaviour of a Unix operating system; systems passing the tests can be branded 'Unix'.
questions.explanation: Linux is not certified Unix (unless a distribution pays for certification).

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: What is the role of the Linux Foundation?
questions.answer: A non‑profit that promotes, protects, and standardises Linux, and hosts the work of Linus Torvalds.
questions.explanation: It also supports open source projects like Kubernetes, Node.js, etc.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: What is the kernel?
questions.answer: The core component of an operating system that manages hardware resources and provides services to user programs.
questions.explanation: It runs in privileged mode (kernel space) and controls CPU, memory, devices, and processes.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: What does process management by the kernel involve?
questions.answer: Creating, scheduling, terminating processes, and handling context switches.
questions.explanation: The scheduler decides which process runs on the CPU and for how long.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: What is virtual memory?
questions.answer: A memory management technique that gives each process its own address space, using disk as an extension of RAM (swap).
questions.explanation: The kernel maps virtual addresses to physical pages and handles page faults.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: How does the kernel manage devices?
questions.answer: Through device drivers that provide a uniform interface to hardware (block devices, character devices, network interfaces).
questions.explanation: Drivers run in kernel space and handle I/O requests.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: What is a system call?
questions.answer: A request from a user‑space program to the kernel for a privileged service (e.g., reading a file).
questions.explanation: System calls are the only way to ask the kernel to perform operations on behalf of a process.

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What is a shell in Unix/Linux?
questions.answer: A command‑line interpreter that accepts user commands, executes them, and displays results.
questions.explanation: It acts as the interface between the user and the kernel.

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What is the Bourne shell (sh)?
questions.answer: The original Unix shell written by Stephen Bourne at Bell Labs; the basis for most modern shells.
questions.explanation: It is still used as `/bin/sh` in many systems (often a symlink to bash or dash).

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What is Bash?
questions.answer: Bourne Again SHell – the default shell on most Linux distributions, extending sh with features like command history, tab completion, and arrays.
questions.explanation: Bash is compatible with sh but adds many interactive conveniences.

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What is Zsh (Z Shell)?
questions.answer: An extended shell with advanced features like spell correction, themeable prompts, plugins, and better globbing.
questions.explanation: Zsh is the default shell on macOS (as of Catalina).

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: How to check which shell you are using?
questions.answer: `echo $SHELL` or `ps -p $$`.
questions.explanation: `$SHELL` shows the login shell; `ps` shows the current process name.

questions.topic: Shell vs Terminal vs Console – clearing the confusion
questions.question: What is the difference between a shell and a terminal?
questions.answer: A terminal (emulator) is a program that provides a text window; a shell is the command interpreter inside it.
questions.explanation: You cannot interact with a shell without a terminal emulator (like GNOME Terminal, iTerm2, or Konsole).

questions.topic: Shell vs Terminal vs Console – clearing the confusion
questions.question: What is a console?
questions.answer: Historically, a physical device (keyboard+screen) directly attached to the computer; now sometimes used as a synonym for terminal.
questions.explanation: In Unix, `/dev/console` is a special device for system messages.

questions.topic: Understanding system utilities and core GNU tools
questions.question: What are core utilities (coreutils)?
questions.answer: A set of basic file, shell, and text manipulation programs (like `ls`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`, `cat`, `grep`, etc.).
questions.explanation: GNU coreutils is the standard implementation on Linux.

questions.topic: Popular Linux distributions and where they are used (Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS)
questions.question: What is Ubuntu best known for?
questions.answer: User‑friendliness, extensive documentation, and regular Long‑Term Support (LTS) releases; popular for desktops and cloud servers.
questions.explanation: Based on Debian, maintained by Canonical.

questions.topic: Popular Linux distributions and where they are used (Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS)
questions.question: What is Debian known for?
questions.answer: Stability, free software philosophy, and its package management (`.deb`).
questions.explanation: Debian is the upstream for Ubuntu and many other distros.

questions.topic: Popular Linux distributions and where they are used (Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS)
questions.question: What is Fedora?
questions.answer: A cutting‑edge distribution sponsored by Red Hat, featuring the latest kernel and software.
questions.explanation: Fedora serves as the testing ground for Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL).

questions.topic: Popular Linux distributions and where they are used (Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS)
questions.question: What is CentOS (now CentOS Stream)?
questions.answer: Previously a free, binary‑compatible rebuild of RHEL; CentOS Stream is a rolling‑release upstream for RHEL.
questions.explanation: Used mostly on servers and enterprise environments.

questions.topic: Desktop vs Server Linux – real-world usage
questions.question: What is the main difference between desktop Linux and server Linux?
questions.answer: Desktop Linux includes a graphical user interface (GUI) and user applications; server Linux typically runs headless (no GUI) and focuses on network services.
questions.explanation: Server Linux uses fewer resources and is optimised for stability and security.

questions.topic: Desktop vs Server Linux – real-world usage
questions.question: Name three real‑world uses of Linux servers.
questions.answer: Web servers (Apache/NGINX), database servers (MySQL/PostgreSQL), cloud infrastructure (AWS EC2).
questions.explanation: The majority of the internet runs on Linux servers.

questions.topic: Opening terminal: shortcuts and interface tour
questions.question: Common keyboard shortcut to open a terminal in many Linux distributions?
questions.answer: `Ctrl+Alt+T` (GNOME, KDE, etc.)
questions.explanation: Some desktops use `Ctrl+Alt+F1` for virtual console; `Ctrl+Alt+T` for terminal emulator.

questions.topic: Basic navigation commands: pwd, ls, cd – with live examples
questions.question: What does `pwd` stand for and what does it do?
questions.answer: Print Working Directory – outputs the absolute path of the current directory.
questions.explanation: Always useful when you forget where you are in the file system.

questions.topic: Basic navigation commands: pwd, ls, cd – with live examples
questions.question: What does `ls` do?
questions.answer: Lists the contents of a directory.
questions.explanation: Common options: `-l` (long format), `-a` (include hidden files), `-h` (human‑readable sizes).

questions.topic: Basic navigation commands: pwd, ls, cd – with live examples
questions.question: What does `cd ..` do?
questions.answer: Moves up one directory level to the parent directory.
questions.explanation: `cd .` stays in same directory, `cd ~` goes to home directory.

questions.topic: Understanding command structure: command, options, arguments
questions.question: What is the general syntax of a Linux command?
questions.answer: `command [options] [arguments]`
questions.explanation: Options usually start with a hyphen (`-r`) or double hyphen (`--recursive`).

questions.topic: Understanding command structure: command, options, arguments
questions.question: What is the difference between an option and an argument?
questions.answer: Options control the behaviour of the command (e.g., `-l`); arguments are the targets the command acts upon (e.g., filename).
questions.explanation: Some commands accept no arguments (e.g., `ls` defaults to current directory).

questions.topic: Case sensitivity in Linux file names
questions.question: Are Linux filenames case‑sensitive?
questions.answer: Yes, `File.txt` and `file.txt` are different files.
questions.explanation: Windows is case‑insensitive, which often confuses beginners moving to Linux.

questions.topic: Using manual pages: man command and --help flag
questions.question: How to get help for a command using `man`?
questions.answer: `man command`, e.g., `man ls`.
questions.explanation: Manual pages are divided into sections (1‑8); use `man 5 passwd` for the file format section.

questions.topic: Using manual pages: man command and --help flag
questions.question: What does `command --help` do?
questions.answer: Displays a brief usage summary and options for that command (GNU convention).
questions.explanation: Not all commands support `--help`, but most do. `man` is the full documentation.

questions.topic: Understanding environment variables – PATH overview
questions.question: What is the `PATH` environment variable?
questions.answer: A colon‑separated list of directories the shell searches for executable programs.
questions.explanation: Example: `echo $PATH` shows `/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin`.

questions.topic: Understanding environment variables – PATH overview
questions.question: How to add a directory to `PATH` temporarily?
questions.answer: `export PATH=$PATH:/my/bin` (effective for the current session).
questions.explanation: For permanent changes, add the line to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.profile`.

questions.topic: Root user vs normal user – concept and importance
questions.question: What is the root user?
questions.answer: The superuser with unrestricted access to all files and system operations (UID 0).
questions.explanation: Root can override all permissions; using it carelessly can damage the system.

questions.topic: Root user vs normal user – concept and importance
questions.question: How to run a single command with root privileges?
questions.answer: `sudo command`.
questions.explanation: `sudo` requires the user to be in the `sudoers` list; it logs the command for auditing.

questions.topic: Understanding shell prompt symbols and working directory display
questions.question: What does the `$` symbol in prompt indicate?
questions.answer: A normal user shell; `#` indicates the root shell.
questions.explanation: In Bash, `PS1` variable defines the prompt; `\$` expands to `$` or `#` depending on effective UID.

questions.topic: Practice Lab: Running your first 10 Linux commands
questions.question: List five basic Linux commands a beginner should know.
questions.answer: `pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `mkdir`, `rm` (or `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, `cat`, `echo`).
questions.explanation: These allow you to navigate, create, delete, and view files.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: What is a common mistake when using `rm`?
questions.answer: Accidentally deleting important files, especially with `rm -rf /` or `rm -rf *` in the wrong directory.
questions.explanation: Always double‑check the path; use `ls` before `rm` and consider using `-i` (interactive) flag.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: Why is it dangerous to run `sudo` without understanding the command?
questions.answer: Because it gives the command full system access, potentially damaging the OS or deleting data.
questions.explanation: Always read the command’s manual before using `sudo`.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: What happens if you forget a space before a command? (e.g., `cd /home`)
questions.answer: Nothing; spaces are required between command and argument. If you write `cd/home`, the shell tries to execute a program named `cd/home` (which doesn't exist).
questions.explanation: Proper syntax: `cd /home` (space needed).

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: Why should you avoid using `rm -rf /` even as a joke?
questions.answer: It recursively deletes all files on the system, making it unbootable.
questions.explanation: Modern systems have some protections, but it's extremely destructive.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: What is the problem with running `chmod 777` on files?
questions.answer: It gives everyone read, write, execute permissions, severely reducing security.
questions.explanation: Use more restrictive permissions like `755` for executables and `644` for data files.

questions.topic: Basic navigation commands: pwd, ls, cd – with live examples
questions.question: How to go directly to your home directory?
questions.answer: `cd` (without arguments) or `cd ~`.
questions.explanation: The `~` character expands to the home directory.

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What does `echo $0` show?
questions.answer: The name of the current shell.
questions.explanation: For example, `bash`, `zsh`, or `sh`.

questions.topic: Opening terminal: shortcuts and interface tour
questions.question: What is the function of `Ctrl+C` in a terminal?
questions.answer: Sends SIGINT (interrupt) to the foreground process, usually terminating it.
questions.explanation: Useful to stop a running command (e.g., `ping google.com`).

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: Name two kernel data structures.
questions.answer: Process Control Block (PCB), file descriptor table, memory management structures (page tables).
questions.explanation: These help the kernel track resources.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What major Unix variant is used on many modern servers besides Linux?
questions.answer: Illumos (derived from OpenSolaris), FreeBSD, and macOS (Darwin).
questions.explanation: Linux dominates but BSD systems are still used.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: Is Android a Linux distribution?
questions.answer: Yes, Android uses the Linux kernel, but with a different userland (Bionic libc, not GNU).
questions.explanation: Many do not call it 'GNU/Linux' because it lacks GNU components.

questions.topic: Understanding system utilities and core GNU tools
questions.question: Which core utility copies files?
questions.answer: `cp`.
questions.explanation: `cp source destination` copies the file; `cp -r` copies directories.

questions.topic: Understanding system utilities and core GNU tools
questions.question: Which core utility moves (or renames) files?
questions.answer: `mv`.
questions.explanation: `mv oldname newname` renames; `mv file dir/` moves.

questions.topic: Understanding system utilities and core GNU tools
questions.question: Which core utility removes files?
questions.answer: `rm`.
questions.explanation: `rm file` deletes a file. Use `rm -r` for directories.

questions.topic: Popular Linux distributions and where they are used (Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS)
questions.question: What is Alpine Linux used for?
questions.answer: Small, security‑oriented distribution often used for containers due to its tiny size.
questions.explanation: Alpine uses `musl libc` and `busybox` instead of GNU coreutils.

questions.topic: Desktop vs Server Linux – real-world usage
questions.question: Which distribution is most common for home desktops?
questions.answer: Ubuntu (with GNOME or KDE), Linux Mint, Fedora, Pop!_OS.
questions.explanation: These focus on user experience and easy installation.

questions.topic: Desktop vs Server Linux – real-world usage
questions.question: Which distribution is popular for cloud servers?
questions.answer: Ubuntu Server, Amazon Linux, Debian, and Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL).
questions.explanation: AWS offers Amazon Linux 2; many use Ubuntu LTS for its stability.

questions.topic: Using manual pages: man command and --help flag
questions.question: How to search within a man page?
questions.answer: Type `/` then the search term, then `n` for next match.
questions.explanation: Works like `less` pager; `q` to quit.

questions.topic: Understanding environment variables – PATH overview
questions.question: How to display all environment variables?
questions.answer: `env` or `printenv`.
questions.explanation: Both show all variables; `printenv PATH` shows just PATH.

questions.topic: Root user vs normal user – concept and importance
questions.question: What does `sudo -i` do?
questions.answer: Gives a root login shell (root's environment, typically root's home directory).
questions.explanation: `sudo -i` is more comprehensive than `sudo -s`.

questions.topic: Root user vs normal user – concept and importance
questions.question: What is the purpose of `passwd` command?
questions.answer: To change a user’s password.
questions.explanation: Root can change any user's password; normal users can change only their own.

questions.topic: Understanding shell prompt symbols and working directory display
questions.question: How to change the shell prompt to show the current directory?
questions.answer: Add `export PS1='\w $ '` to `~/.bashrc` (`\w` shows full path).
questions.explanation: Many distributions already show the current directory by default.

questions.topic: Practice Lab: Running your first 10 Linux commands
questions.question: What command creates an empty file?
questions.answer: `touch newfile.txt`.
questions.explanation: `touch` also updates timestamps of existing files.

questions.topic: Practice Lab: Running your first 10 Linux commands
questions.question: What command displays the contents of a text file?
questions.answer: `cat file.txt` (or `less`, `more`, `head`).
questions.explanation: `cat` dumps whole file to screen; `less` allows scrolling.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: What is the mistake of forgetting `sudo` when editing system files?
questions.answer: You get 'Permission denied' because system files are owned by root.
questions.explanation: Use `sudo nano /etc/hosts` or `sudo vi /etc/fstab`.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: Why is it undesirable to run `rm *` when you meant `rm *.tmp`?
questions.answer: `*` matches all files, deleting everything in the directory.
questions.explanation: Always use `ls` to check the glob pattern before deletion.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: What happens if you use double quotes instead of single quotes around a string with `$`?
questions.answer: The shell expands variables inside double quotes; single quotes preserve literal text.
questions.explanation: `echo '$HOME'` prints `$HOME`; `echo "$HOME"` prints the path.

questions.topic: Basic navigation commands: pwd, ls, cd – with live examples
questions.question: How to list all files, including hidden ones?
questions.answer: `ls -a` or `ls -al`.
questions.explanation: Hidden files start with `.` (dot).

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: What is the default login shell in most Linux distributions?
questions.answer: Bash (Bourne‑again shell).
questions.explanation: Some distributions (like Ubuntu) have switched to Dash for `/bin/sh` but still default to Bash for interactive users.

questions.topic: Opening terminal: shortcuts and interface tour
questions.question: What does `Ctrl+L` do in a terminal?
questions.answer: Clears the screen (similar to `clear`).
questions.explanation: It redraws the terminal, keeping the prompt at the top.

questions.topic: Using manual pages: man command and --help flag
questions.question: How to exit a man page?
questions.answer: Press `q`.
questions.explanation: Man pages use the `less` pager, which quits with `q`.

questions.topic: Understanding environment variables – PATH overview
questions.question: What is the typical order of directories in `PATH`?
questions.answer: `/usr/local/bin`, `/usr/bin`, `/bin`, possibly `/usr/sbin` and `/sbin` for root.
questions.explanation: The order determines precedence when commands have the same name.

questions.topic: Root user vs normal user – concept and importance
questions.question: What is the `whoami` command?
questions.answer: Displays the current effective username.
questions.explanation: Useful after `sudo` to check if you are still root.

questions.topic: Common beginner mistakes in terminal usage
questions.question: Why does `command not found` appear even when a program is installed?
questions.answer: The program might not be in the `PATH`, or you need `sudo` to run it (if in `/usr/sbin`).
questions.explanation: Check with `which command` or `type command`.

questions.topic: Evolution of UNIX – Bell Labs to modern Linux
questions.question: What is the role of the Open Group in Unix?
questions.answer: It holds the UNIX trademark and certifies operating systems that conform to the Single UNIX Specification.
questions.explanation: MacOS and a few commercial Unices are certified; Linux is not.

questions.topic: Difference between UNIX, Linux and GNU/Linux
questions.question: What is the kernel of an operating system?
questions.answer: The core component that handles hardware, processes, and memory; Linux is a kernel.
questions.explanation: GNU/Linux = Linux kernel + GNU tools; Android = Linux kernel + Android runtime.

questions.topic: Role of the kernel: process, memory and device management (conceptual)
questions.question: What is a process control block (PCB)?
questions.answer: A kernel data structure containing information about a process (PID, state, registers, etc.).
questions.explanation: In Linux, it's `task_struct`.

questions.topic: What is a shell? Bash vs Zsh vs Sh
questions.question: How to change your default shell?
questions.answer: `chsh -s /bin/zsh` (needs to be added to `/etc/shells`).
questions.explanation: `chsh` changes the login shell; you must log out and back in.

questions.topic: Practice Lab: Running your first 10 Linux commands
questions.question: How to create a new directory?
questions.answer: `mkdir newdir`.
questions.explanation: Use `mkdir -p` to create parent directories as needed.
